## WHAT HAPPENS AFTER SGD REACHES ZERO LOSS? –A MATHEMATICAL FRAMEWORK

## **Zhiyuan Li**

Department of Computer Science Princeton University zhiyuanli@cs.princeton.edu

## **Tianhao Wang**

Department of Statistics and Data Science Yale University tianhao.wang@yale.edu

## **Sanjeev Arora**

Department of Computer Science Princeton University arora@cs.princeton.edu

## ABSTRACT

Understanding the implicit bias of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is one of the key challenges in deep learning, especially for overparametrized models, where the local minimizers of the loss function _L_ can form a manifold. Intuitively, with a sufficiently small learning rate _η_ , SGD tracks Gradient Descent (GD) until it gets close to such manifold, where the gradient noise prevents further convergence. In such regime, Blanc et al. (2020) proved that SGD with label noise locally decreases a regularizer-like term, the sharpness of loss, tr[ _∇_[2] _L_ ]. The current paper gives a general framework for such analysis by adapting ideas from Katzenberger (1991). It allows in principle a complete characterization for the regularization effect of SGD around such manifold—i.e., the ”implicit bias”—using a stochastic differential equation (SDE) describing the limiting dynamics of the parameters, which is determined jointly by the loss function and the noise covariance. This yields some new results: (1) a _global_ analysis of the implicit bias valid for _η[−]_[2] steps, in contrast to the local analysis of Blanc et al. (2020) that is only valid for _η[−]_[1] _[.]_[6] steps and (2) allowing _arbitrary_ noise covariance. As an application, we show with arbitrary large initialization, label noise SGD can always escape the kernel regime and only requires _O_ ( _κ_ ln _d_ ) samples for learning an _κ_ -sparse overparametrized linear model in R _[d]_ (Woodworth et al., 2020), while GD initialized in the kernel regime requires Ω(� _d_ ) samples. This upper bound is minimax optimal and improves the previous _O_ ( _κ_[2] ) upper bound (HaoChen et al., 2020).

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The implicit bias underlies the generalization ability of machine learning models trained by stochastic gradient descent (SGD). But it still remains a mystery to mathematically characterize such bias. We study SGD in the following formulation

where _η_ is the learning rate (LR), _L_ : R _[D] →_ R is the training loss and _σ_ ( _x_ ) = [ _σ_ 1( _x_ ) _, σ_ 2( _x_ ) _, . . . , σ_ Ξ( _x_ )] _∈_ R _[D][×]_[Ξ] is a deterministic noise function. Here _ξk_ is sampled uniformly from _{_ 1 _,_ 2 _, . . . ,_ Ξ _}_ and it satisfies E _ξk_ [ _σξk_ ( _x_ )] = 0, _∀x ∈_ R _[d]_ and _k_ .

It is widely believed that large LR (or equivalently, small batch size) helps SGD find better minima. For instance, some previous works argued that large noise enables SGD to select a flatter attraction basin of the loss landscape which potentially benefits generalization (Li et al., 2019c; Jastrzebski et al., 2017). However, there is also experimental evidence (Li et al., 2020b) that small LR also has equally good implicit bias (albeit with higher training time), and that is the case studied here. Presumably low LR precludes SGD jumping between different basins since under general conditions this should require Ω(exp(1 _/η_ )) steps (Shi et al., 2020). In other words, there should be a mechanism to reach better generalization while staying within a single basin. For deterministic GD similar mechanisms

Recent work (Blanc et al., 2020) shed light on this direction by analyzing effects of stochasticity in the gradient. For sufficiently small LR, SGD will reach and be trapped around some manifold of local minimizers, denoted by Γ (see Figure 2). The effect is shown to be an implicit deterministic drift in a direction corresponding to lowering a regularizer-like term along the manifold. They showed SGD with label noise locally decreases the sharpness of loss, tr[ _∇_[2] _L_ ], by Θ( _η_[0] _[.]_[4] ) in _η[−]_[1] _[.]_[6] steps. However, such an analysis is actually _local_ , since the natural time scale of analysis should be _η[−]_[2] , not _η[−]_[1] _[.]_[6] .
The contribution of the current paper is a more general and global analysis of this type. We introduce a more powerful framework inspired by the classic paper (Katzenberger, 1991).

## 1.1 INTUITIVE EXPLANATION OF REGULARIZATION EFFECT DUE TO SGD

We start with an intuitive description of the implicit regularization effect described in Blanc et al. (2020). For simplification, we show it for the canonical SDE approximation (See Section B.1 for more details) of SGD (1) (Li et al., 2017; Cheng et al., 2020). Here _W_ ( _t_ ) is the standard Ξ-dimensional Brownian motion. The only property about label noise SGD we will use is that the noise covariance _σσ[⊤]_ ( _x_ ) = _∇_[2] _L_ ( _x_ ) for every _x_ in the manifold Γ (See derivation in Section 5).

Suppose _X_[˜] _η_ (0) is already close to some local minimizer point _X∗ ∈_ Γ. The goal is to show _X_[˜] _η_ ( _t_ ) will move in the tangent space and steadily decrease tr[ _∇_[2] _L_ ]. At first glance, this seems impossible as the gradient _∇L_ vanishes around Γ, and the noise has zero mean, implying SGD should be like random walk instead of a deterministic drift. The key observation of Blanc et al. (2020) is that the local dynamics of _X_[˜] _η_ ( _t_ ) is completely different in tangent space and normal space — the fast random walk in normal space causes _X_[˜] _η_ ( _t_ ) to move slowly (with velocity Θ( _η_[2] )) but deterministically in certain direction. To explain this, letting ∆( _t_ ) = _X_[˜] _η_ ( _t_ ) _− X∗_ , Taylor expansion of (2) gives d∆( _t_ ) _≈−η∇_[2] _L_ ( _X∗_ )∆d _t_ + _ησ_ ( _X∗_ )d _W_ ( _t_ ), meaning ∆ is behaving like an Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (OU) process locally in the normal space. Its mixing time is Θ( _η[−]_[1] ) and the stationary distribution is the standard multivariate gaussian in the normal space scaled by _[√] η_ (see Figure 1b), because noise covariance _σσ[⊤]_ = _∇_[2] _L_ . Though this OU process itself doesn’t form any regularization, it activates the second order Taylor expansion of _∇L_ ( _X∗_ + ∆( _t_ )), i.e., _−_[1] 2 _[∂]_[2][(] _[∇][L]_[)(] _[X][∗]_[)[∆(] _[t]_[)] _[,]_[ ∆(] _[t]_[)]][, creating] a Θ( _η_[2] ) velocity in the tangent space. Since there is no push back force in the tangent space, the small velocity accumulates over time, and in a longer time scale of Ω( _η[−]_[1] ), the time average of the stochastic velocity is roughly the same as the expected velocity when ∆ is sampled from its stationary distribution. This simplifies the expression of the velocity in tangent space to _[η]_ 2[2] _[∇][T]_[tr[] _[∇]_[2] _[L]_[]][ (see] Figure 1c), where _∇T_ means the gradient is only taken in the tangent space.

However, the above approach only gives a local analysis for _O_ ( _η[−]_[1] _[.]_[6] ) time, where the total movement due to implicit regularization is _O_ ( _η_[2] _[−]_[1] _[.]_[6] ) = _O_ ( _η_[0] _[.]_[4] ) and thus is negligible when _η →_ 0. In order to get a non-trivial limiting dynamics when _η →_ 0, a global analysis for Ω( _η[−]_[2] ) steps is necessary and it cannot be done by Taylor expansion with a single reference point. Recent work by Damian et al. (2021) glues analyses of multiple local phases into a global guarantee that SGD finds a ( _ϵ, γ_ )- stationary point for the regularized loss, but still doesn’t show convergence for trajectory when _η →_ 0 and cannot deal with general noise types, _e.g._ , noise lying in the tangent space of the manifold. ## 1.2 OUR APPROACH: SEPARATING THE SLOW FROM THE FAST

In this work, we tackle this problem via a different angle. First, since the anticipated limiting dynamics is of speed Θ( _η_[2] ), we change the time scaling to accelerate (2) by _η[−]_[2] times, which yields

The key idea here is that we only need to track the slow dynamic, or equivalently, some _projection_ of _X_ onto the manifold Γ, Φ( _X_ ). Here Φ : R _[D] →_ Γ is some function to be specified and hopefully we can simplify the dynamics (3) via choosing suitable Φ. To track the dynamics of Φ( _Xη_ ), we apply Ito’s lemma (a.k.a. stochastic chain rule, see Lemma A.9) to Equation (3), which yields

Note the first term _−η[−]_[1] _∂_ Φ( _Xη_ ) _∇L_ ( _Xη_ ) is going to diverge to _∞_ when _η →_ 0, so a natural choice for Φ is to kill the first term. Further note _−∂_ Φ( _X_ ) _∇L_ ( _X_ ) is indeed the directional derivative of Φ at _X_ towards _−∇L_ , killing the first term becomes equivalent to making Φ invariant under Gradient Flow (GF) of _−∇L_ ( _X_ )! Thus it suffices to take Φ( _X_ ) to be the limit of GF starting at _X_ . (Formally defined in Section 3; see Lemma C.2 for a proof of _∂_ Φ( _X_ ) _∇L_ ( _X_ ) _≡_ 0.)
Also intuitively _Xη_ will be infinitely close to Γ, i.e., _d_ ( _Xη_ ( _t_ ) _,_ Γ) _→_ 0 for any _t >_ 0 as _η →_ 0, so we have Φ( _Xη_ ) _≈ Xη_ . Thus we can rewrite the above equation as

The above argument for SDE was first formalized and rigorously proved by Katzenberger (1991). It included an extension of the analysis to the case of asymptotic continuous dynamics (Theorem 4.1) including SGD with infinitesimal LR, but the result is weaker in this case and no convergence is shown. Another obstacle for applying this analysis is that 2nd order partial derivatives of Φ are unknown. We solve these issues in Section 4 and our main result Theorem 4.6 gives a clean and complete characterization for the implicit bias of SGD with infinitesimal LR in Θ( _η[−]_[2] ) steps. Finally, our Corollary 5.2 shows (5) gives exactly the same regularization as tr[ _∇_[2] _L_ ] for label noise SGD.

The **main contributions** of this paper are summarized as follows.

1. In Section 4, we propose a mathematical framework to study the implicit bias of SGD with infinitesimal LR. Our main theorem (Theorem 4.6) gives the limiting diffusion of SGD with LR _η_ for Θ( _η[−]_[2] ) steps as _η →_ 0 and allows any covariance structure.

> 2. In Section 5, we give limiting dynamics of SGD with isotropic noise and label noise.

> 3. In Section 6, we show for any initialization, SGD with label noise achieves _O_ ( _κ_ ln _d_ ) sample complexity for learning a _κ_ -sparse overparametrized linear model (Woodworth et al., 2020). In this case, the implicit regularizer is a data-dependent weighted _ℓ_ 1 regularizer, meaning noise can help reduce the norm and even escape the kernel regime. The _O_ ( _κ_ ln _d_ ) rate is minimax optimal (Raskutti et al., 2012) and improves over _O_[˜] ( _κ_[2] ) upper bound by HaoChen et al. (2020). In contrast, vanilla GD requires Ω( _d_ ) samples to generalize in the kernel regime. For technical contributions, we rigorously prove the convergence of GF for OLM (Lemma 6.3), unlike many existing implicit bias analyses which have to assume the convergence. We also prove the convergence of limiting flow to the global minimizer of the regularizer (Lemma 6.5) by a trajectory analysis via our framework. It cannot be proved by previous results (Blanc et al., 2020; Damian et al., 2021), as they only assert convergence to stationary point in the best case.

## 2 RELATED WORKS

**Loss Landscape of Overparametrized Models** A phenomenon known as mode connectivity has been observed that local minimizers of the loss function of a neural network are connected by simple paths (Freeman & Bruna, 2016; Garipov et al., 2018; Draxler et al., 2018), especially for overparametrized models (Venturi et al., 2018; Liang et al., 2018; Nguyen et al., 2018; Nguyen, 2019). Later this phenomanon is explained under generic assumptions by Kuditipudi et al. (2019). Moreover, it has been proved that the local minimizers of an overparametrized network form a low-dimensional manifold (Cooper, 2018; 2020) which possibly has many components. Fehrman et al. (2020) proved the convergence rate of SGD to the manifold of local minimizers starting in a small neighborhood.

**Implicit Bias in Overparametrized Models** Algorithmic regularization has received great attention in the community (Arora et al., 2018; 2019a; Gunasekar et al., 2018b;a;b; Soudry et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018; 2020a). In particular, the SGD noise is widely believed to be a promising candidate for explaining the generalization ability of modern neural networks (LeCun et al., 2012; Keskar et al., 2016; Hoffer et al., 2017; Zhu et al., 2018; Li et al., 2019a). Beyond the size of noise (Li et al., 2019c; Jastrzebski et al., 2017), the shape and class of the noise also play an important role (Wen et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2020). It is shown by HaoChen et al. (2020) that parameter-dependent noise will bias SGD towards a low-complexity local minimizer. Similar implicit bias has also been studied for overparametrized nonlinear statistical models by Fan et al. (2020). Several existing works (Vaskevicius et al., 2019; Woodworth et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2019) have shown that for the quadratically overparametrized linear model, i.e., _w_ = _u[⊙]_[2] _−v[⊙]_[2] or _w_ = _u⊙v_ , gradient descent/flow from small initialization implicitly regularizes _ℓ_ 1 norm and provides better generalization when the groundtruth is sparse. This is in sharp contrast to the kernel regime, where neural networks trained by gradient descent behaves like kernel methods (Daniely, 2017; Jacot et al., 2018; Yang, 2019). This allows one to prove convergence to zero loss solutions in overparametrized settings (Li & Liang, 2018; Du et al., 2018; Allen-Zhu et al., 2019b;a; Du et al., 2019; Zou et al., 2020), where the learnt function minimizes the corresponding RKHS norm (Arora et al., 2019b; Chizat et al., 2018).

**Modelling Stochastic First-Order Methods with Itˆo SDE** Apart from the discrete-time analysis, another popular approach to study SGD is through the continuous-time lens using SDE (Li et al., 2017; 2019b; Cheng et al., 2020). Such an approach is often more elegant and can provide fruitful insights like the linear scaling rule (Krizhevsky, 2014; Goyal et al., 2017) and the intrinsic learning rate (Li et al., 2020b). A recent work by Li et al. (2021) justifies such SDE approximation. Xie et al. (2020) gave a heuristic derivation explaining why SGD favors flat minima with SDE approximation. Wojtowytsch (2021) showed that the invariant distribution of the canonical SDE approximation of SGD will collapse to some manifold of minimizers and in particular, favors flat minima. By approximating SGD using a SDE with slightly modified covariance for the overparametrized linear model, Pesme et al. (2021) relates the strength of implicit regularization to training speed.

## 4.3 MAIN RESULT

Now we are ready to present our main result. It’s a direct combination of Theorem B.9 and Lemma 4.5. **Theorem 4.6.** _Suppose the loss function L, the manifold of local minimizer_ Γ _and the open neighborhood U satisfy Assumptions 3.1 and 3.2, and xη_ (0) = _x_ (0) _∈ U for all η >_ 0 _. Based on the above theorem, the limiting dynamics of SGD can be understood as follows: (a) the **tangent noise** , Σ[1] _∥[/]_[2] ( _Y_ )d _W_ ( _t_ ), is preserved, and the second term of (10) can be viewed as the necessary **tangent noise compensation** for the limiting dynamics to stay on Γ. Indeed, Lemma C.7 shows that the value of the second term only depends on Γ itself, i.e., it’s same for all loss _L_ which locally defines the same Γ. (b) The noise in the normal space is killed since the limiting dynamics always stay on Γ. However, its second order effect (Ito correction term) takes place as a vector fieldˆ on Γ, which induces the **Noise Regularization** and **Mixed Regularization** term, corresponding to the mixed and normal noise covariance respectively.

**Remark 4.7.** _In Appendix B.4 we indeed prove a stronger version of Theorem 4.6 that the sample paths of SGD converge in distribution, i.e., let x_ ˜ _η_ ( _t_ ) = _xη_ ( _⌊t/η_[2] _⌋_ ) _, then x_ ˜ _η weakly converges to Y on_ [0 _, T_ ] _. Moreover, we only assume the existence of a global solution for ease of presentation. ## 6.1 LIMITING FLOW CONVERGES TO MINIMIZERS OF REGULARIZER

In this subsection we show limiting flow (13) starting from anywhere on Γ converges to the minimizer of regularizer _R_ (by Lemma 6.5). The proof contains two parts: (a) the limiting flow converges; (b) the limit point of the flow cannot be sub-optimal stationary points. These are indeed the most technical and difficult parts of proving the _O_ ( _κ_ ln _d_ ) upper bound, where the difficulty comes from the fact that the manifold Γ is not compact, and the stationary points of the limiting flow are in fact all located on the boundary of Γ. However, the limiting flow itself is not even defined on the boundary of the manifold Γ. Even if we can extend _∂_ Φ( _·_ ) _∇R_ ( _·_ ) continuously to entire R _[D]_ , the continuous extension is not everywhere differentiable.
Thus the non-compactness of Γ brings challenges for both (a) and (b). For (a), the convergence for standard gradient flow is often for free, as long as the trajectory is bounded and the objective is

However, since our flow does not satisfy those nice properties, we have to show that the limiting flow satisfies Polyak-Łojasiewicz condition (a special case of KL condition) (Polyak, 1964) via careful calculation (by Lemma D.16).

For (b), the standard analysis based on center stable manifold theorem shows that gradient descent/flow converges to strict saddle (stationary point with at least one negative eigenvalue in hessian) only for a zero-measure set of initialization (Lee et al., 2016; 2017). However, such analyses cannot deal with the case where the flow is not differentiable at the sub-optimal stationary point. To circumvent this issue, we prove the non-convergence to sub-optimal stationary points with a novel approach: we show that for any stationary point _x_ , whenever there exists a descent direction of the regularizer _R_ at _x_ , we can construct a potential function which increases monotonically along the flow around _x_ , while the potential function is equal to _−∞_ at _x_ , leading to a contradiction. ## 7 CONCLUSION AND FUTURE WORK

We propose a mathematical framework to study the implicit bias of SGD with infinitesimal LR. We show that with arbitrary noise covariance, Θ( _η[−]_[2] ) steps of SGD converge to a limiting diffusion on certain manifold of local minimizer, as the LR _η →_ 0. For specific noise types, this allows us to recover and strengthen results regarding implicit bias in previous works with much simpler analysis. In particular, we show a sample complexity gap between label noise SGD and GD in the kernel regime for a overparametrized linear model, justifying the generalization benefit of SGD. For the future work, we believe our framework can be applied to analyze the implicit bias of SGD in more complex models towards better understanding of the algorithmic regularization induced by stochasticity. It will be valuable to extend our method to other stochastic optimization algorithms, e.g., ADAM, SGD with momentum.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper proposes a continuous-time SDE framework using the Katzenberger (1991) approach to analyze the implicit bias of SGD in the overparameterized regime. By re-scaling time and focusing on the slow dynamics on the manifold of minimizers, the authors derive a limiting diffusion process that characterizes the implicit regularizer for arbitrary noise covariance structures and provide a global analysis valid for O(eta^-2) steps.

The paper provides a mathematically rigorous and elegant framework for characterizing SGD's implicit bias, successfully extending local analyses to a global regime. Its application to overparameterized linear models provides a compelling theoretical justification for the generalization benefits of label noise. The work is a significant contribution to the understanding of SGD dynamics in deep learning.

Overview issues:

- Unclear assumptions on the existence of the limiting SDE global solution: Theorem 4.6 relies on the existence of a global solution Y(t) to the limiting SDE (10) that remains within the neighborhood U for all t > 0. While the authors acknowledge this in Remark 4.7, they do not provide sufficient conditions under which this holds for general loss landscapes. In high-dimensional non-convex settings, trajectories can easily encounter singular points or leave the neighborhood of a specific manifold. The paper should explicitly define the regularity conditions on the Hessian or the manifold geometry that guarantee the stability of this limiting SDE.

- Discrepancy in the treatment of non-compact manifolds: In Section 6, the authors address the non-compactness of the manifold Gamma when proving the convergence of the limiting flow to the regularizer's minimizer. They invoke a potential function argument to handle stationary points on the boundary. However, the connection between the SDE (10) and the deterministic flow (17) in this non-compact setting lacks a detailed justification regarding the behavior of the noise term near the boundary. A more rigorous treatment of the boundary behavior of the limiting diffusion is needed to ensure that the convergence results hold as the trajectory approaches the boundary of the manifold.

- Limited discussion on the transition from discrete-time SGD to SDE: The framework assumes that the discrete-time SGD iterates can be modeled as a Katzenberger process. While Lemma 4.2 establishes this mapping, the convergence of the discrete-time noise sequence to the Brownian motion in the SDE is a delicate step. The paper relies on the user-friendly interface (Theorem B.9) to bypass the technical difficulties of the original Katzenberger theorem, but the conditions required for this interface (e.g., the convergence of stopping times) are not explicitly verified for the specific noise structures discussed. Providing a more concrete verification of these conditions for a standard neural network architecture would strengthen the validity of the continuous-time approximation.

- Need for empirical validation of the limiting SDE predictions: The paper provides a strong theoretical framework but lacks numerical simulations to demonstrate the 'bite' of the derived SDE. While the theoretical results are robust, including a simple numerical experiment—such as tracking the trajectory of SGD on a toy overparameterized linear model and comparing it to the simulated trajectories of the limiting SDE (10)—would provide necessary validation. This would bridge the gap between the abstract mathematical derivation and the actual behavior of SGD, confirming that the second-order terms derived in Equation (10) are indeed the dominant factors in the implicit regularization.

- Numerical verification of the limiting SDE trajectory: The paper derives a limiting SDE (10) for SGD iterates but lacks a numerical simulation to demonstrate that this SDE accurately captures the trajectory of discrete-time SGD. While the theoretical derivation is elegant, readers need to see that the second-order terms in the SDE actually govern the long-term behavior of SGD in a concrete setting. I suggest adding a simulation using the Overparametrized Linear Model (OLM) described in Section 6, where the trajectory of the discrete-time SGD iterates is plotted against paths generated by numerically integrating the derived SDE (10). This would confirm that the theoretical framework has practical predictive power for the implicit bias.

- Explicit conditions for the stability of the limiting SDE: Theorem 4.6 assumes the existence of a global solution to the limiting SDE (10) that remains within the neighborhood U for all time. In high-dimensional non-convex landscapes, this is a strong assumption that is not guaranteed by the provided definitions. The paper would benefit from explicitly stating the regularity conditions on the loss function L—such as growth conditions at the boundary of U or specific spectral properties of the Hessian—that ensure the limiting SDE does not diverge or exit the manifold. Providing these conditions would clarify the scope of the framework and prevent the result from appearing vacuous in settings where the manifold is not globally well-behaved.

Recommendation: minor revision. The paper is technically strong and addresses a significant problem in the field with a novel framework. The revision should focus on clarifying the stability conditions for the limiting SDE and providing a simple numerical validation to demonstrate the practical relevance of the derived diffusion process.

Revision targets:

- Include a numerical simulation comparing the trajectory of SGD on a small-scale linear model to the trajectories generated by the derived limiting SDE.

- Explicitly state the regularity conditions on the loss landscape (beyond C^3 smoothness) required to ensure the limiting SDE remains well-posed and stable.

- Provide a more detailed justification for the convergence of the discrete-time noise sequence, specifically addressing the validity of the stopping time conditions used in Theorem 4.6.

comments.title: Inconsistency in the sample complexity gap claim
comments.quote: In particular, we show a sample complexity gap between label noise SGD and GD in the kernel regime for a overparametrized linear model, justifying the generalization benefit of SGD.
comments.explanation: The abstract and introduction suggest the sample complexity gap occurs within the kernel regime. However, your analysis in Section 6 demonstrates that the benefit of label noise SGD arises precisely because it escapes the kernel regime. Please rephrase this to clarify that the gap is a consequence of SGD's ability to leave the kernel regime, rather than a performance difference within it.

comments.title: Triviality of the lower bound for n >= 2
comments.quote: the expected $\ell_{2}$ loss of $x(T)$ is at least $(1-\frac{n}{2})\left\|w^{*}\right\|_{2}^{2}$
comments.explanation: The lower bound (1 - n/2) ||w*||_2^2 becomes trivial (zero or negative) for n >= 2. Since the goal is to establish an Omega(d) sample complexity requirement, this bound does not support the claim. Please re-derive the expectation to show the intended dependence on d.

comments.title: Inconsistency in OU process mixing time scaling
comments.quote: ixing time is  $\Theta(\eta^{-1})$  and the stationary distribution is the standard multivariate gaussian in the normal space scaled by  $\sqrt{\eta}$
comments.explanation: The mixing time logic requires clarification. For the process dDelta(t) = -eta * nabla^2 L * Delta dt + eta * sigma dW(t), the mixing time is Theta(1) in the rescaled time tau = eta * t, which corresponds to Theta(eta^-1) in original time. Please explicitly mention this time-scale transformation to connect the mixing time to the Theta(eta^2) drift velocity.

comments.title: Inconsistency in pseudo-determinant definition
comments.quote: nabla^{2}L(Y)|_{+}$ is also equal to the sum of log of non-zero eigenvalue values of $\nabla^{2}L(Y)$.
comments.explanation: The sum of the logarithms of the non-zero eigenvalues is the log-pseudo-determinant, ln |nabla^2 L(Y)|_+, not the pseudo-determinant itself. Please correct this terminology to avoid confusion with the expression in equation (11).

The paper is theoretically ambitious and makes a significant contribution to understanding SGD's implicit bias.

Overview issues:

- Katzenberger's theorem assumptions are not verified for SGD dynamics: The paper relies heavily on Katzenberger's theorem (Theorem 4.1) to derive the limiting SDE, but it does not verify that the assumptions of this theorem hold for the SGD dynamics considered. Katzenberger's theorem requires conditions such as smoothness, boundedness, and ergodicity of the stochastic process, as well as convergence of the stopped processes. The paper acknowledges this difficulty in Section 4.1 but does not provide a rigorous verification. Instead, it presents Theorem B.9 as a 'user-friendly interface' that only requires information about the limiting diffusion. However, the assumptions of Theorem B.9 itself are not explicitly stated or verified for the SGD case. Without this verification, the derivation of the limiting SDE (10) is not fully justified. The authors should either explicitly verify the conditions of Katzenberger's theorem or provide a self-contained proof that does not rely on external results.

- Separation of timescales is not rigorously justified: The paper's central idea is to separate fast dynamics in the normal space from slow dynamics in the tangent space. While the intuitive explanation in Section 1.1 is clear, the rigorous justification is lacking. The argument that the OU process in the normal space mixes quickly and that its stationary distribution activates second-order terms relies on the assumption that the manifold is an attracting invariant set. However, the paper does not prove that the manifold is indeed attracting for the SGD dynamics, nor does it quantify the rate of attraction. The analysis in Section 4.2 derives closed-form expressions for the derivatives of Φ, but these expressions are only valid on Γ. The paper needs to show that the SGD dynamics stay close to Γ for long enough times and that the approximation errors are controlled. A rigorous justification would involve proving that the normal component of the dynamics converges to a stationary distribution and that the tangent component can be averaged over this distribution.

- The closed-form expression for the limiting SDE is not validated against known results: The paper claims that the limiting SDE (10) provides a complete characterization of SGD's implicit bias, but it does not validate this expression against known results for simple cases. For example, for a quadratic loss with a unique minimizer, the manifold Γ is a single point, and the limiting SDE should reduce to a simple diffusion. The paper does not check whether (10) reproduces this trivial case correctly. Similarly, for a linear model with label noise, the limiting flow (13) should be consistent with the results of Blanc et al. (2020) and Damian et al. (2021). While the paper claims that Corollary 5.2 recovers these results, it does not provide a detailed comparison or show that the derived expression matches the known explicit formulas. Validating the framework on simple, analytically tractable examples would increase confidence in its correctness and help identify potential errors in the derivation.

- The assumption that SGD reaches exactly zero loss may be violated in practice: The entire analysis assumes that SGD reaches exactly zero loss and stays on the manifold of global minimizers. However, in practice, numerical precision and non-convex landscapes may prevent the loss from reaching exactly zero. The paper does not discuss how its results would be affected if the loss only reaches a small positive value. Moreover, the assumption that the manifold Γ consists of global minimizers may be too strong; in many deep learning settings, the model may not interpolate the data exactly, and the minimizers may only be local. The paper should address the robustness of its results to approximate zero loss and discuss whether the framework can be extended to the case where the loss is only near zero.

- The claimed generalization benefit with label noise relies on hidden assumptions: Section 6 claims a provable generalization benefit of label noise SGD over GD in the kernel regime for overparametrized linear models. However, the proof relies on several assumptions that may not hold in practice. First, it assumes that the data distribution is either i.i.d. Gaussian or Boolean, which is a strong restriction. Second, it assumes that the groundtruth is exactly κ-sparse, which may not be realistic. Third, the lower bound for GD (Theorem 6.7) is derived for a linearized model (NTK regime), but the paper does not show that this lower bound holds for the original nonlinear model. The comparison between SGD and GD is therefore not on equal footing: the SGD upper bound is for the nonlinear model, while the GD lower bound is for the linearized model. The paper should either prove a lower bound for GD on the nonlinear model or acknowledge that the comparison is not direct.

- The analysis assumes a smooth, connected manifold of global minimizers, which may not exist: Assumption 3.1 requires that the set of local minimizers Γ is a smooth submanifold of dimension D-M. While the paper argues that this holds for overparametrized models under a full-rank condition on the Jacobian, this condition may fail in practice. For example, the manifold may have singularities where the Jacobian drops rank, or it may consist of multiple disconnected components. The paper does not discuss how its results would be affected if Γ is not a smooth manifold. Moreover, the assumption that gradient flow converges to Γ (Assumption 3.2) is strong and may not hold for all initializations. The paper should discuss the limitations of these assumptions and provide evidence that they are satisfied for the models considered.

- The framework does not handle noise covariance that varies along the manifold: The limiting SDE (10) depends on the noise covariance Σ(x) and its decomposition into tangent and normal components. However, the paper assumes that Σ(x) is known and well-defined for all x in Γ. In practice, the noise covariance may vary along the manifold, and its decomposition may not be smooth. The paper does not discuss the regularity conditions required for Σ(x) to be differentiable or how the results would change if Σ(x) is not constant. Moreover, the derivation of the closed-form expression for ∂²Φ[Σ] (Lemma 4.5) assumes that Σ is symmetric and that the Lyapunov operator is invertible, which may not hold for all noise types. The paper should clarify the conditions under which the expression is valid and discuss potential extensions to non-smooth or degenerate noise covariances.

- The paper lacks a simulation or numerical example demonstrating the theory: Despite being a theoretical paper, the claims about the limiting SDE and the generalization benefit would be greatly strengthened by a simple simulation or numerical example. For instance, the authors could simulate SGD on the overparametrized linear model with label noise and compare the trajectory to the predicted limiting flow (17). Such an experiment would demonstrate that the framework has practical bite and that the assumptions (e.g., small learning rate, long time horizon) are not too restrictive. Without any empirical validation, the reader is left wondering whether the theoretical results are merely asymptotic and whether they hold for realistic learning rates and step counts. A simple Monte Carlo simulation would address this concern and make the paper more convincing.

- No verification of Katzenberger's theorem assumptions for SGD: The paper relies on Katzenberger's theorem (Theorem 4.1) to derive the limiting SDE, but it never verifies that the assumptions of this theorem hold for the SGD dynamics. The authors acknowledge this difficulty in Section 4.1 and present Theorem B.9 as a 'user-friendly interface,' yet the assumptions of Theorem B.9 itself are not explicitly stated or verified for the SGD case. Without this verification, the derivation of the limiting SDE (10) is not fully justified. The authors should either explicitly verify the conditions of Katzenberger's theorem (e.g., smoothness, boundedness, ergodicity of the stochastic process, convergence of stopped processes) or provide a self-contained proof that does not rely on external results. A natural starting point would be to verify these conditions for the overparametrized linear model used in Section 6, which is already a concrete setting where the loss and noise are well-defined.

- No validation of limiting SDE against simple known cases: The paper claims that the limiting SDE (10) provides a complete characterization of SGD's implicit bias, but it never validates this expression against simple, analytically tractable examples. For instance, for a quadratic loss with a unique minimizer, the manifold Γ is a single point, and the limiting SDE should reduce to a trivial diffusion (no drift, no noise). The paper does not check whether (10) reproduces this case correctly. Similarly, for a linear model with label noise, the limiting flow (13) should be consistent with the results of Blanc et al. (2020) and Damian et al. (2021), but the paper does not provide a detailed comparison or show that the derived expression matches known explicit formulas. Validating the framework on such simple cases would increase confidence in its correctness and help identify potential errors in the derivation. The authors should compute the limiting SDE for a quadratic loss in one dimension and verify that it matches the known behavior of SGD near a unique minimizer.

- GD lower bound is for linearized model, not nonlinear model: Section 6 claims a sample complexity gap between label noise SGD and GD in the kernel regime for overparametrized linear models. However, the lower bound for GD (Theorem 6.7) is derived for a linearized model (NTK regime), while the SGD upper bound is for the original nonlinear model. The paper does not show that the lower bound holds for the nonlinear model, nor does it discuss whether the linearization is a valid approximation for the GD dynamics. This makes the comparison between SGD and GD not on equal footing: the SGD upper bound is for the nonlinear model, while the GD lower bound is for the linearized model. The authors should either prove a lower bound for GD on the nonlinear model (e.g., by showing that GD in the kernel regime cannot escape to the rich regime) or explicitly acknowledge that the comparison is indirect and discuss the limitations of the linearization assumption.

- No discussion of how to estimate the limiting SDE in practice: The paper derives a theoretical limiting SDE (10) for SGD with infinitesimal learning rate, but it provides no practical guidance on how to estimate the quantities involved in this SDE for real models. For instance, the SDE depends on the projection matrix ∂Φ, the Hessian ∇²L, and the noise covariance Σ, all of which are high-dimensional objects that are difficult to compute or approximate in practice. The paper does not discuss whether these quantities can be estimated from data, nor does it address the curse of dimensionality that arises when conditioning on the manifold Γ. A paper that claims practical relevance (e.g., explaining why SGD generalizes better than GD) should at least sketch how the framework could be applied to real neural networks, perhaps by discussing approximations or by noting that the framework provides qualitative insights rather than quantitative predictions. The authors should add a paragraph in Section 7 discussing the practical challenges of applying the framework and suggesting potential avenues for approximation.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Verify the assumptions of Katzenberger's theorem for the SGD dynamics, or provide a self-contained proof of the limiting SDE that does not rely on external results.

- Add a rigorous justification of the separation of timescales, including a proof that the normal dynamics converge to a stationary distribution and that the tangent dynamics can be averaged.

- Validate the closed-form expression for the limiting SDE (10) against known results for simple cases (e.g., quadratic loss, linear model with label noise) and provide a detailed comparison.

- Discuss the robustness of the results to approximate zero loss and the conditions under which the manifold assumptions hold, including potential singularities or disconnected components.

- Include a simulation or numerical example demonstrating the theory, such as a comparison of SGD trajectories to the predicted limiting flow for the overparametrized linear model.

comments.title: Assumption of exactly zero loss may be violated in practice
comments.quote: Understanding the implicit bias of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) is one of the key challenges in deep learning, especially for overparametrized models, where the local minimizers of the loss function $L$ can form a manifold.
comments.explanation: The entire analysis assumes that SGD reaches exactly zero loss and stays on the manifold of global minimizers. However, in practice, numerical precision and non-convex landscapes may prevent the loss from reaching exactly zero. The paper does not discuss how its results would be affected if the loss only reaches a small positive value. Moreover, the assumption that the manifold Γ consists of global minimizers may be too strong; in many deep learning settings, the model may not interpolate the data exactly, and the minimizers may only be local. The paper should address the robustness of its results to approximate zero loss and discuss whether the framework can be extended to the case where the loss is only near zero.

comments.title: Noise covariance assumption not explicitly stated
comments.quote: This yields some new results: (1) a global analysis of the implicit bias valid for $\eta^{-2}$ steps, in contrast to the local analysis of *Blanc et al. (2020)* that is only valid for $\eta^{-1.6}$ steps and (2) allowing arbitrary noise covariance.
comments.explanation: The paper claims to allow arbitrary noise covariance, but the derivation of the limiting SDE (10) in Section 4 relies on the noise covariance being of the form Σ(θ) = (1/η) * Cov(∇ℓ_i(θ)) (from the SGD noise). The paper does not explicitly state whether the 'arbitrary noise covariance' refers to the covariance of the gradient noise or the label noise, nor does it discuss constraints (e.g., positive definiteness, boundedness) needed for the SDE limit to exist. Without this clarification, the claim of 'arbitrary noise covariance' is misleading. The authors should specify the exact form of the noise covariance assumed in the framework and discuss any restrictions.

comments.title: Global analysis time scale not justified
comments.quote: This yields some new results: (1) a global analysis of the implicit bias valid for $\eta^{-2}$ steps, in contrast to the local analysis of *Blanc et al. (2020)* that is only valid for $\eta^{-1.6}$ steps and (2) allowing arbitrary noise covariance.
comments.explanation: The paper claims a global analysis valid for η^{-2} steps, but the derivation in Section 4 relies on a local expansion around the manifold Γ and assumes that the dynamics stay close to Γ for that duration. The paper does not provide a rigorous bound on how long the approximation remains valid, nor does it prove that the SGD dynamics do not escape the neighborhood of Γ within η^{-2} steps. Without such a bound, the claim of 'global analysis' is not substantiated. The authors should provide a rigorous estimate of the time horizon over which the SDE approximation holds, or at least state the assumptions needed.

comments.title: Lyapunov operator invertibility on W_H needs proof
comments.quote: For a symmetric matrix $H$, we define $W_{H}=\{\Sigma\in\mathbb{R}^{D\times D}\mid\Sigma=\Sigma^{\top},HH^{\dagger}\Sigma=\Sigma=\Sigma HH^{\dagger}\}$ and Lyapunov Operator $\mathcal{L}_{H}:W_{H}\to W_{H}$ as $\mathcal{L}_{H}(\Sigma)=H^{\top}\Sigma+\Sigma H$. It’s easy to verify $\mathcal{L}_{H}^{-1}$ is well-defined on $W_{H}$.
comments.explanation: The Lyapunov equation HΣ + ΣH = C has a unique solution for every C iff λ_i + λ_j ≠ 0 for all eigenvalue pairs of H. Since H = ∇²L(x) is positive semidefinite with zero eigenvalues on Γ, the condition fails on the full space. The restriction to W_H is meant to address this, but the paper merely asserts invertibility without proof. The authors should provide a proof or cite a known result: for symmetric H, L_H is invertible on the subspace of symmetric matrices that commute with H and whose column space is contained in the column space of H. The definition of W_H ensures Σ is supported on the range of H, but one must also verify that L_H maps W_H bijectively onto itself. Without this, the use of L_{∇²L}^{-1}(Σ_⟂) in (9) is not fully justified.

comments.title: Unverified existence of global SDE solution
comments.quote: If SDE (10) has a global solution $Y$ with $Y(0)=x(0)$ and $Y$ never leaves $U$, i.e., $\mathbb{P}[Y(t)\in U,\forall t\geq 0]=1$, then for any $T>0$, $x_{\eta}(\lfloor T/\eta^{2}\rfloor)$ converges in distribution to $Y(T)$ as $\eta\to 0$.

The SDE involves ∇²L(Y)† and L_{∇²L}^{-1}, which may blow up if ∇²L is not invertible on Γ or if the Lyapunov operator is not invertible. Moreover, the drift and diffusion coefficients may not satisfy standard Lipschitz or linear growth conditions. The authors should either prove global existence under the given assumptions or state additional conditions (e.g., compactness of Γ, boundedness of derivatives) that guarantee it. Without this, the theorem's conclusion is conditional on an unverified premise.

comments.title: Groundtruth recovery claim not proven in section

The proof roadmap lists Lemma 6.6 as showing that the minimizer of the regularizer recovers the groundtruth, but Lemma 6.6 is not stated or proven in this section. The text only defines x* and asserts convergence without argument. The authors should either include the statement of Lemma 6.6 and a brief justification, or explicitly reference the appendix where the proof is given. This is a missing step in the logical flow.

comments.title: Assumption 3.2 verification incomplete
comments.quote: We verify that the above loss function $L$ and manifold $\Gamma$ satisfy Assumption 3.1 by Lemma 6.2, and that the neighborhood $U$ and $\Gamma$ satisfy Assumption 3.2 by Lemma 6.3.
comments.explanation: The paper claims Lemma 6.3 verifies Assumption 3.2, which requires Φ(U) ⊂ Γ. However, Lemma 6.3 states: 'For gradient flow dx/dt = -∇L(x) starting at any x0 ∈ U, it holds that Φ(x0) ∈ Γ.' This is a statement about the limit of gradient flow, not about the map Φ defined in the general framework. The paper does not define Φ explicitly for this setting, nor does it show that the limit of gradient flow coincides with the projection Φ used in the general theory. Without this connection, the verification of Assumption 3.2 is incomplete. The authors should clarify the definition of Φ and how Lemma 6.3 implies Φ(U) ⊂ Γ.

comments.title: Uniqueness claim needs clarification on ambiguity
comments.quote: n}$, the optimal solution of (18), $(\hat{u},\hat{v})$, is unique up to sign flips of each coordinate and recovers the groundtruth, i.e., $\hat{u}^{\odot 2}-\hat{v}^{\odot 2}=w^{*}$.
comments.explanation: The regularizer R(x) in (18) depends on u_j^2 + v_j^2, which is not invariant under adding a constant vector c to both u and v. However, the paper's claim is that the solution is unique 'up to sign flips of each coordinate' — this refers to the fact that flipping the sign of both u_j and v_j simultaneously leaves u_j^2 - v_j^2 unchanged but changes u_j^2 + v_j^2. The shift ambiguity (u_j + c, v_j + c) would change u_j^2 + v_j^2 and thus R(x), so it would not preserve optimality. The paper should explicitly argue why the shift ambiguity cannot arise at the minimizer, perhaps because the regularizer penalizes large values of u_j^2 + v_j^2 and the constraint fixes u_j^2 - v_j^2. Without this argument, the uniqueness claim is incomplete.

comments.title: Missing justification for equivalence to ℓ1 minimization
comments.quote: Note $\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}z_{i,j}^{2}=1$ when $z_{i,j}\overset{iid}{\sim}\operatorname{Unif}\{-1,1\}$, and we can show minimizing $R(x)$ on $\Gamma$, (18), is equivalent to finding the minimum $\ell_{1}$ norm solution of Equation (14).
comments.explanation: The paper claims that minimizing R(x) on Γ is equivalent to finding the minimum ℓ1 norm solution of Equation (14), but provides no derivation. The regularizer R(x) in (18) is a weighted ℓ2 norm on (u, v), not an ℓ1 norm. The equivalence would require a specific relationship between the weights and the ℓ1 norm of w = u^{⊙2} - v^{⊙2}. The paper should either provide a brief derivation or cite a known result. Without this, the claim that standard sparse recovery results apply is unsupported.

comments.title: Gaussian case claim lacks proof or reference
comments.quote: The gaussian case is more complicated but still can be proved with techniques from *Tropp (2015)*.
comments.explanation: The paper asserts that the Gaussian case can be proved using techniques from Tropp (2015), but does not specify which techniques or how they apply. Tropp (2015) covers concentration inequalities and random matrix theory, but the connection to the specific optimization problem (18) is not obvious. The paper should either provide a sketch of the proof or cite a more specific result (e.g., a theorem number) from Tropp (2015) that directly applies. Without this, the claim is vague and unverifiable.

comments.title: Negative lower bound is trivial
comments.quote: Then for any groundtruth $w^{*}$, any learning rate schedule $\{\eta_{t}\}_{t\geq 1}$, and any fixed number of steps $T$, the expected $\ell_{2}$ loss of $x(T)$ is at least $(1-\frac{n}{2})\left\|w^{*}\right\|_{2}^{2}$, where $x(T)$ is the $T$-th iterate of GD on $L$, i.e., $x(t+1)=x(t)-\eta_{t}\nabla L(x(t))$, for all $t\geq 0$.
comments.explanation: The lower bound $(1-n/2)\|w^*\|_2^2$ becomes negative for $n>2$, which is trivially true since the loss is nonnegative. This provides no meaningful information. The authors should either correct the expression (e.g., it may be $(1-2/n)$ or involve $\max(0,1-n/2)$) or provide a derivation showing why this particular form arises. Without clarification, the claimed lower bound is not informative and may be incorrect.

comments.title: Bound does not support Ω(d) claim
comments.quote: In this subsection we show GD needs at least $\Omega(d)$ samples to learn OLM, when initialized in the kernel regime. This lower bound holds for all learning rate schedules and numbers of steps. This is in sharp contrast to the $\widetilde{O}(\kappa\ln d)$ sample complexity upper bound of SGD with label noise.
comments.explanation: The theorem states a lower bound of $(1-n/2)\|w^*\|_2^2$, which does not involve dimension $d$ at all. To claim an $\Omega(d)$ sample complexity, the theorem should derive a condition on $n$ relative to $d$ (e.g., if $n \leq c d$ then the loss is at least something). The current bound does not provide such a threshold. The authors should either modify the theorem to include a dimension-dependent condition or adjust the surrounding text to accurately reflect what the theorem shows.

comments.title: Linearized model lower bound not justified
comments.quote: For ease of illustration, we directly consider the lower bound for test loss when the NTK is fixed throughout the training.
comments.explanation: The theorem considers the loss with a linearized model $L(x)=\sum_{i=1}^n(f_i(x_0)+\langle\nabla f_i(x_0), x-x_0\rangle - y_i)^2$, which fixes the NTK at initialization. However, the surrounding text claims this lower bound applies to GD on the original nonlinear model in the kernel regime. The paper does not justify that the linearized approximation is valid for the lower bound — i.e., that the true GD loss is at least as large as the linearized loss. In general, the nonlinear model could achieve lower loss than the linearized one, so a lower bound on the linearized model does not automatically imply a lower bound on the original model. The authors should either prove that the linearized loss lower bounds the original loss under the kernel regime assumptions, or explicitly state that the lower bound is for the linearized model only and adjust the claims accordingly.

comments.title: Bound does not depend on T or η_t
comments.quote: Then for any groundtruth $w^{*}$, any learning rate schedule $\{\eta_{t}\}_{t\geq 1}$, and any fixed number of steps $T$, the expected $\ell_{2}$ loss of $x(T)$ is at least $(1-\frac{n}{2})\left\|w^{*}\right\|_{2}^{2}$, where $x(T)$ is the $T$-th iterate of GD on $L$, i.e., $x(t+1)=x(t)-\eta_{t}\nabla L(x(t))$, for all $t\geq 0$.
comments.explanation: The lower bound does not depend on the number of steps $T$ or the learning rates $\eta_t$, which is suspicious because GD with many steps or carefully tuned learning rates could potentially achieve lower loss. The theorem claims the bound holds for any $T$ and any schedule, but without a derivation, it is unclear how this can be true. The authors should provide a proof sketch or reference showing why the bound is independent of these parameters, or clarify that the bound is a worst-case over all possible schedules and steps.

comments.title: Inconsistent dimension notation in SGD formulation
comments.quote:
$x_{\eta}(k+1)=x_{\eta}(k)-\eta(\nabla L(x_{\eta}(k))+\sqrt{\Xi}\cdot\sigma_{\xi_{k}}(x_{\eta}(k)))$ (1)

where $\eta$ is the learning rate (LR), $L:\mathbb{R}^{D}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}$ is the training loss and $\sigma(x)=[\sigma_{1}(x),\sigma_{2}(x),\dots,\sigma_{\mathbb{E}}(x)]\in\mathbb{R}^{D\times\mathbb{E}}$ is a deterministic noise function. Here $\xi_{k}$ is sampled uniformly from $\{1,2,\dots,\mathbb{E}\}$ and it satisfies $\mathbb{E}_{\xi_{k}}[\sigma_{\xi_{k}}(x)]=0$, $\forall x\in\mathbb{R}^{d}$ and $k$.
comments.explanation: The domain of $x$ is given as $\mathbb{R}^{D}$ in the definition of $L$, but the expectation condition states $\forall x\in\mathbb{R}^{d}$ with a lowercase $d$. This is an inconsistency: either the ambient dimension is $D$ or $d$, but not both. The paper should use a single symbol consistently. Rewrite the expectation condition as $\forall x\in\mathbb{R}^{D}$ to match the loss definition.

comments.title: Unclear definition of noise scaling factor
comments.quote:
$x_{\eta}(k+1)=x_{\eta}(k)-\eta(\nabla L(x_{\eta}(k))+\sqrt{\Xi}\cdot\sigma_{\xi_{k}}(x_{\eta}(k)))$ (1)

where $\eta$ is the learning rate (LR), $L:\mathbb{R}^{D}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}$ is the training loss and $\sigma(x)=[\sigma_{1}(x),\sigma_{2}(x),\dots,\sigma_{\mathbb{E}}(x)]\in\mathbb{R}^{D\times\mathbb{E}}$ is a deterministic noise function.
comments.explanation: The symbol $\Xi$ appears in the noise scaling $\sqrt{\Xi}$ but is never defined in the provided text. It is unclear whether $\Xi$ is a scalar, a matrix, or something else. If $\Xi$ is a scalar, the product $\sqrt{\Xi}\cdot\sigma_{\xi_{k}}(x)$ is dimensionally consistent because $\sigma_{\xi_{k}}(x)$ is a vector in $\mathbb{R}^{D}$, so $\sqrt{\Xi}$ should be a scalar. If $\Xi$ is a matrix, the notation $\sqrt{\Xi}$ typically denotes a matrix square root, but this is not specified. The authors should define $\Xi$ explicitly (e.g., as a scalar noise magnitude or a covariance matrix) to avoid ambiguity.

comments.title: Unused term Σ_{∥,⟂} in decomposition
comments.quote: Then $\Sigma$ can be decomposed as $\Sigma=\Sigma_{\parallel}+\Sigma_{\perp}+\Sigma_{\parallel,\perp}+\Sigma_{\perp,\parallel}$, where $\Sigma_{\parallel}:=\partial\Phi\Sigma\partial\Phi$, $\Sigma_{\perp}:=(I_{D}-\partial\Phi)\Sigma(I_{D}-\partial\Phi)$ and $\Sigma_{\parallel,\perp}=\Sigma_{\perp,\parallel}^{\top}=\partial\Phi\Sigma(I_{D}-\partial\Phi)$ are the noise covariance in tangent space, normal space and across both spaces, respectively.
comments.explanation: $\Sigma_{\parallel,\perp}$ is defined but never used in (9). This is correct: (9) uses $\Sigma_\parallel$, $\Sigma_\perp$, and $\Sigma_{\perp,\parallel}$, but not $\Sigma_{\parallel,\perp}$. Since $\Sigma_{\parallel,\perp} = \Sigma_{\perp,\parallel}^\top$, the term $\Sigma_{\parallel,\perp}$ is implicitly the transpose of the used term, but the formula does not involve it directly. This is not an error, but it creates confusion. The authors should either remove the definition of $\Sigma_{\parallel,\perp}$ or add a remark explaining that only $\Sigma_{\perp,\parallel}$ appears because the expression is not symmetric in the cross terms.

comments.title: Label noise covariance equality needs on-manifold qualifier
comments.explanation:
The equality $\Sigma(x) = \delta^2 
abla^2 L(x)$ holds only on the manifold $\Gamma$ where $f_i(x) = y_i$ for all $i$, because the Hessian of $L$ contains a term $ rac{1}{n}\sum_i (f_i(x)-y_i)
abla^2 f_i(x)$ that vanishes only on $\Gamma$. The paper later states that $\Gamma$ is a subset of $\{x \mid f_i(x)=y_i\}$, but the quoted sentence does not explicitly restrict to $x \in \Gamma$. Since the SDE (10) is defined on $\Gamma$, this is a minor omission but could confuse readers. Add a clarifying phrase: 'For $x \in \Gamma$, we have $\Sigma(x) = \delta^2 
abla^2 L(x)$ because the second term in the Hessian vanishes.'

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes a continuous-time SDE framework to characterize the implicit bias of SGD on zero-loss manifolds, leveraging Katzenberger’s projection theorem to derive a limiting diffusion valid over Θ(η⁻²) steps. The theoretical machinery is elegant and extends prior local analyses to a global regime, with a concrete application to sparse recovery in overparameterized linear models. However, the framework rests on several restrictive assumptions, lacks quantification of finite-step-size errors, and omits empirical validation. Addressing the mathematical rigor around the diffusion coefficients, time-scale separation, and practical scaling would substantially strengthen the work.

This paper makes a meaningful theoretical contribution by unifying the analysis of SGD’s implicit bias under arbitrary noise covariance and extending the time horizon from η⁻¹·⁶ to η⁻² steps. The derivation of the limiting diffusion on the minimizer manifold is mathematically sophisticated, and the sparse recovery application demonstrates clear algorithmic advantages over deterministic gradient descent. The main limitation lies in the gap between asymptotic theory and practical training dynamics, particularly regarding the verification of Katzenberger’s regularity conditions, the fragility of fast-slow dynamics near flat regions, and the absence of numerical validation.

Overview issues:

- Overly Restrictive Manifold and Regularity Assumptions: Assumption 3.1 requires the zero-loss set to be a C² submanifold with constant Hessian rank M, while Assumption 3.2 demands global gradient flow convergence within a neighborhood U. Realistic overparameterized networks frequently exhibit degenerate critical points, non-smooth loss surfaces, or disconnected minimizer components that violate these conditions. The framework provides no mechanism to handle manifolds with varying curvature or singularities, which limits its applicability beyond idealized linear models. Readers will wonder how the theory translates to architectures where the Hessian rank drops or the loss landscape contains sharp valleys. The authors should either relax these assumptions to allow piecewise-smooth manifolds or explicitly characterize the failure modes when the rank condition breaks down.

- Unquantified Finite-Step-Size Error in the Diffusion Limit: Theorem 4.6 establishes convergence only in the asymptotic regime η → 0 over Θ(η⁻²) steps. The paper treats the continuous-time SDE (10) as the exact limiting behavior but never bounds the discretization error between the discrete SGD update (1) and the SDE solution for practical learning rates. Mini-batch noise scaling and step-size decay schedules, which are standard in practice, are not incorporated into the error analysis. Without a finite-step-size bound, it remains unclear how many steps are actually needed before the implicit bias manifests, or how learning rate warmup disrupts the assumed time-scale separation. Adding a rigorous discretization error bound or a simulation comparing discrete SGD trajectories to the predicted SDE would ground the asymptotic results.

- Insufficient Verification of Katzenberger’s Theorem Conditions: The derivation in Section 4.3 directly applies Katzenberger’s projection theorem to derive the limiting diffusion (10), yet it sidesteps the regularity and non-degeneracy requirements that the original theorem imposes on the drift and diffusion coefficients. Specifically, the noise covariance Σ(x) = σ(x)σ(x)ᵀ must satisfy boundedness and ellipticity conditions on Γ to guarantee the diffusion is well-posed and the projection dynamics are stable. The paper assumes a global solution to (10) exists and never leaves U (Theorem 4.6), but does not prove that Σ∥ and Σ⊥ meet the technical thresholds needed for the theorem to hold. The authors need to explicitly verify these coefficient conditions for their chosen noise models or restrict the theorem to regimes where they are guaranteed.

- Fragility of Fast-Slow Dynamics Near Degenerate Regions: The entire regularization mechanism relies on a clean separation between fast normal mixing (timescale η⁻¹) and slow tangent drift (timescale η⁻²), as outlined in Section 1.1. This separation collapses when the Hessian eigenvalues in the normal space approach zero, which is common near flat minima or in highly non-convex regions. The paper does not analyze how the limiting flow (10) behaves when the spectral gap vanishes, nor does it provide bounds on the mixing time that depend on the minimum non-zero eigenvalue. If the normal dynamics slow down, the Itô correction terms in (10) may not average out as assumed, invalidating the derived drift. A sensitivity analysis or a modified framework that accounts for vanishing spectral gaps would strengthen the theoretical claims.

- Unaddressed Scaling of Generalization Bounds with Model Width and Batch Size: Theorem 6.1 claims an O(κ ln d) sample complexity for sparse recovery using label noise SGD, but the derivation assumes a fixed noise level δ and does not track how the bound scales with the overparameterization ratio D/n or the mini-batch size. In practice, label noise magnitude and effective noise covariance depend heavily on batch size and model width, which directly influence the regularization strength in Corollary 5.2. The paper omits a discussion on how these hyperparameters affect the tightness of the generalization bound or whether the O(κ ln d) rate holds when the noise covariance becomes rank-deficient due to small batches. Including a dimensionality analysis or a table showing bound dependence on D, n, and batch size would clarify the practical utility of the result.

- Lack of Numerical Validation for the Predicted Limiting Dynamics: The framework derives a closed-form SDE for the limiting diffusion but provides no numerical experiments to verify that finite-step SGD actually follows the predicted trajectory. A Monte Carlo study comparing SGD iterates to the solution of (10) under varying learning rates, noise types, and initialization scales would demonstrate whether the asymptotic theory captures real training dynamics. Without empirical validation, the connection between the abstract manifold projection and practical implicit bias remains untested. The authors should include a simulation section showing trajectory alignment, convergence rates, and sensitivity to η to substantiate the theoretical claims.

- Concrete computation of the general limiting drift: The general SDE (10) introduces several distinct drift components—Tangent Noise Compensation, Mixed Regularization, and Normal Regularization—but the paper only evaluates these for isotropic and label noise. Readers cannot gauge how these terms interact without a worked example. Compute equation (10) for a simple two-dimensional loss, such as L(x,y) = (x^2 - y)^2 + λy^2 with a known zero-loss manifold, and explicitly derive the resulting drift vector field. This would let readers verify whether the Mixed Regularization term actually counteracts the Normal Regularization term in practice. Such a computation would ground the abstract projection formulas and make the theoretical claims tangible.

- Practical guidance for estimating manifold derivatives: The framework's applicability hinges on computing ∂Φ and ∂²Φ on the zero-loss manifold. Lemma 4.5 expresses these derivatives using the pseudo-inverse of the Hessian and the Lyapunov operator, but the paper offers no discussion on how to estimate or approximate these quantities for high-dimensional models. Practitioners would need to know whether these derivatives can be consistently estimated from discrete SGD trajectories, or if finite-difference approximations are feasible. Adding a brief discussion on the computational tractability of these terms, or a method to estimate the limiting drift from finite-step data, would address a major implementation gap. Without this, the framework remains a purely theoretical construct with unclear utility for empirical validation.

- Behavior under highly anisotropic noise covariance: The authors claim the framework handles arbitrary noise covariance, yet the analysis stops at isotropic and label noise. Real SGD noise is notoriously anisotropic, often concentrated along specific data directions. Demonstrate the limiting dynamics for a specific anisotropic DGP where the noise covariance Σ is rank-deficient or misaligned with the Hessian. Show how the trajectory behaves when the Mixed Regularization term becomes large relative to the Normal Regularization term. This would prove that the framework does not merely recover prior results but actually predicts novel bias patterns under realistic noise structures. A concrete example here would validate the generality of the main theorem.

- Formal reduction to prior local analysis: The introduction positions the framework as a global extension of Blanc et al. (2020), which derived a local sharpness-descent dynamics valid for Θ(η⁻¹·⁶) steps. The paper does not formally show how the global SDE (10) collapses to the local drift ½η²∇_T tr[∇²L] when restricted to a small neighborhood on Γ. Deriving this local limit from the general equation would validate the framework against established benchmarks and clarify the mathematical bridge between the short-term and long-term regimes. Providing this reduction would strengthen the paper's positioning and help readers connect the new theory to existing literature.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Verify and explicitly state the boundedness, non-degeneracy, and regularity conditions required by Katzenberger’s theorem for the drift and diffusion coefficients in Section 4.3, or restrict Theorem 4.6 to parameter regimes where they hold.

- Derive a finite-step-size error bound quantifying the distance between discrete SGD trajectories (1) and the limiting SDE (10) for practical learning rates, and discuss how step-size decay or warmup schedules affect the asymptotic limit.

- Analyze the robustness of the fast-slow time-scale separation when normal-space Hessian eigenvalues approach zero, providing mixing-time bounds that depend on the spectral gap or proposing a modified flow for degenerate regions.

- Include a Monte Carlo simulation comparing finite-step SGD dynamics to the predicted limiting diffusion across different noise covariances, learning rates, and initialization scales to validate the theoretical framework.

- Extend the generalization analysis in Section 6 to explicitly track how the O(κ ln d) bound scales with model width D, sample size n, and mini-batch size, clarifying the practical conditions under which the sparse recovery guarantee holds.

comments.title: SDE noise scaling contradicts standard SGD diffusion limit
comments.quote: d} \tilde {X} _ {\eta} (t) = - \eta \nabla L (\tilde {X} _ {\eta} (t)) \mathrm {d} t + \eta \cdot \sigma (\tilde {X} _ {\eta} (t)) \mathrm {d} W (t).
comments.explanation: The noise coefficient $\eta$ in Equation (2) contradicts standard diffusion approximations for SGD. Discrete SGD with learning rate $\eta$ accumulates variance proportional to $\eta$ per unit time, which requires a $\sqrt{\eta}$ scaling on the Brownian motion term. Using $\eta$ artificially shrinks the noise, leading to an $O(\sqrt{\eta})$ stationary distribution and altering the drift order. Rewrite the noise term as $\sqrt{\eta} \cdot \sigma(\tilde{X}_\eta(t)) \mathrm{d}W(t)$ to align with established literature, and adjust the subsequent stationary distribution and drift calculations accordingly.

comments.title: Undefined scaling factor $\Xi$ in SGD update
comments.quote: $x_{\eta}(k+1)=x_{\eta}(k)-\eta(\nabla L(x_{\eta}(k))+\sqrt{\Xi}\cdot\sigma_{\xi_{k}}(x_{\eta}(k)))$
comments.explanation: The symbol $\Xi$ appears as a noise scaling factor in Equation (1) but is never defined in the surrounding text or notation section. Standard formulations tie this scaling to the batch size or noise covariance structure. Define $\Xi$ explicitly or replace it with the intended parameter to ensure the update rule is fully specified before deriving the diffusion limit.

comments.title: Projection map regularity exceeds manifold smoothness
comments.quote: Assume that $U$ is an open neighborhood of $\Gamma$ satisfying that gradient flow starting in $U$ converges to some point in $\Gamma$, i.e., $\forall x\in U$, $\Phi(x)\in\Gamma$. (Then $\Phi$ is $\mathcal{C}^{2}$ on $U$ by *Falconer (1983)*.)
comments.explanation: The claim that $\Phi$ is $\mathcal{C}^2$ on $U$ is mathematically inconsistent with Assumption 3.1, which only guarantees $\Gamma$ is a $\mathcal{C}^2$ submanifold. For a $\mathcal{C}^k$ manifold, the metric projection or flow-based retraction is generally only $\mathcal{C}^{k-1}$. Additionally, Falconer (1983) focuses on fractal geometry and does not apply to smooth manifold projections. The authors should either downgrade the regularity claim for $\Phi$ to $\mathcal{C}^1$, upgrade $L$ to $\mathcal{C}^4$ and $\Gamma$ to $\mathcal{C}^3$, or cite a standard reference on stable manifold projections.

comments.title: Missing uniform spectral gap assumption
comments.quote: Assumption 3.1. Assume that the loss $L:\mathbb{R}^{D}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}$ is a $\mathcal{C}^{3}$ function, and that $\Gamma$ is a $(D-M)$-dimensional $\mathcal{C}^{2}$-submanifold of $\mathbb{R}^{D}$ for some integer $0\leq M\leq D$, where for all $x\in\Gamma$, $x$ is a local minimizer of $L$ and $\mathrm{rank}(\nabla^{2}L(x))=M$.
comments.explanation: The rank condition $\mathrm{rank}(\nabla^{2}L(x))=M$ ensures exactly $M$ positive eigenvalues on $\Gamma$, but it does not prevent the smallest positive eigenvalue from approaching zero if $\Gamma$ is non-compact. This degeneracy breaks the smoothness of the projection map $\Phi$ and invalidates the uniform convergence rates needed for the averaging argument. Impose a uniform spectral gap condition $\lambda_{\min}(\nabla^2 L(x)|_{N_x\Gamma}) \geq c > 0$ for all $x \in \Gamma$, or explicitly restrict the analysis to compact manifolds.

comments.title: Degenerate noise covariance in Lemma 4.2
comments.quote: where $\Sigma\equiv\sigma\sigma^{\top}$ and $\{W(t)\}_{t\geq 0}$ is a $\Xi$-dimensional standard Brownian motion.
comments.explanation: The constructed process $Z_n(t)$ uses uniform categorical sampling, which yields a covariance matrix $I - \frac{1}{\Xi}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^\top$ with rank $\Xi-1$. Consequently, $Z_n(t)$ converges to a Brownian motion confined to a subspace, not a full-rank standard Brownian motion. This rank deficiency invalidates the direct application of Theorem 4.1 and requires the Itô correction term in Equation (8) to incorporate the projection matrix $P = I - \frac{1}{\Xi}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^\top$. Adjust the noise description and the limiting SDE to reflect the constrained covariance structure.

comments.title: Sign error and vanishing term in Eq. (9)
comments.quote:
$\partial^{2}\Phi[\Sigma]=\!(
abla^{2}L)^{\dagger}\partial^{2}(
abla L)\left[\Sigma_{\parallel}ight]-\partial\Phi\partial^{2}(
abla L)\left[\mathcal{L}_{

comments.explanation:
Differentiating the constraint $
abla L(\Phi(x)) = 0$ twice yields $H \partial^2 \Phi[v, w] = -D^3 L(\partial\Phi v, \partial\Phi w)$, where $H = 
abla^2 L(\Phi(x))$. Applying $H$ to the first term of Equation (9) gives $H H^\dagger D^3 L(\partial\Phi, \partial\Phi)$, which retains the positive sign from the paper but contradicts the required negative sign from the chain rule. Furthermore, the third term vanishes because $(
abla^2 L)^\dagger$ maps into $	ext{range}(H)$ while $\Sigma_{\perp,\parallel}$ maps into $	ext{null}(H)$. Rewrite Equation (9) with a negative sign on the first term and remove the third term.

comments.title: Incorrect claim regarding term dependence on loss geometry
comments.quote: Indeed, Lemma C.7 shows that the value of the second term only depends on $\Gamma$ itself, i.e., it’s same for all loss $L$ which locally defines the same $\Gamma$.
comments.explanation:
The second drift term in Equation (10) is $ rac{1}{2}
abla^{2}L(Y)^{\dagger}\partial^{2}(
abla L)(Y)\left[\Sigma_{\parallel}(Y)ight]$. While the geometric factors $
abla^{2}L(Y)^{\dagger}$ and $\partial^{2}(
abla L)(Y)$ are intrinsic to $\Gamma$, the term explicitly contains $\Sigma_{\parallel}(Y)$, the projection of the SGD noise covariance onto the tangent space. This covariance depends on the stochastic gradient noise model rather than solely on the loss landscape geometry. Clarify that only the geometric component depends on $\Gamma$, while the full compensation term scales with the tangent noise covariance.

comments.title: Incorrect definition of pseudo-determinant
comments.quote: $|\nabla^{2}L(Y)|_{+}$ is also equal to the sum of log of non-zero eigenvalue values of $\nabla^{2}L(Y)$.
comments.explanation: The pseudo-determinant is defined as the product of the non-zero eigenvalues of a matrix. Taking the logarithm of this product yields the sum of the logarithms of the non-zero eigenvalues. The text incorrectly equates the pseudo-determinant itself to the sum of logs. Rewrite the statement to clarify that $\ln|\nabla^{2}L(Y)|_{+}$ equals the sum of the logarithms of the non-zero eigenvalues.

comments.title: Vacuous negative lower bound in Theorem 6.7
comments.quote: Then for any groundtruth $w^{*}$, any learning rate schedule $\{\eta_{t}\}_{t\geq 1}$, and any fixed number of steps $T$, the expected $\ell_{2}$ loss of $x(T)$ is at least $(1-\frac{n}{2})\left\|w^{*}\right\|_{2}^{2}$
comments.explanation: The bound $(1-n/2)\|w^*\|_2^2$ becomes negative for $n \ge 2$, rendering it vacuous for a non-negative quantity like loss or prediction error. Furthermore, the phrase "expected $\ell_2$ loss" is ambiguous. Since $L(x)$ is quadratic, GD converges to a global minimizer where $L(x) \to 0$. The theorem likely intends to bound the prediction error or parameter error. Rewrite the bound as $(1 - \frac{n}{2d})\|w^*\|_2^2$ (or the correct dimension-dependent fraction) and explicitly define whether the $\ell_2$ quantity refers to prediction error or parameter error.

comments.title: Mischaracterization of Blanc et al. (2020) noise assumption
comments.quote: They showed SGD with label noise locally decreases the sharpness of loss,  $\mathrm{tr}[\nabla^2 L]$ , by  $\Theta (\eta^{0.4})$  in  $\eta^{-1.6}$  steps.
comments.explanation: This sentence attributes the analysis of 'label noise' to Blanc et al. (2020), but that work explicitly analyzes SGD driven by isotropic Gaussian noise. The current paper treats isotropic noise and label noise as distinct cases, with the label noise analysis being a novel contribution. Correct the introduction to state that Blanc et al. (2020) considers isotropic noise, and clarify that extending the framework to label noise is a key result of this paper.

comments.title: Domain restriction excludes the target sparse solution
comments.quote: $\Gamma=\left\{x=(u^{\top},v^{\top})^{\top}\in U:Z(u^{\odot 2}-v^{\odot 2})=Y\right\},\quad\text{where }U=(\mathbb{R}\setminus\{0\})^{D}.$
comments.explanation: The manifold $\Gamma$ is explicitly defined as a subset of $U = (\mathbb{R} \setminus \{0\})^{2d}$, which strictly forbids any coordinate from being zero. However, the claimed target solution $x^*$ has $d-\kappa$ zero entries because the ground truth $w^*$ is $\kappa$-sparse. Consequently, $x^* \notin U$ and $x^* \notin \Gamma$. The limiting flow cannot converge to a point outside its defined domain, and the manifold structure degenerates at the boundary. Rewrite the definition of $U$ in Equation (15) as $U=\mathbb{R}^{D}$ and add a boundary analysis to handle the rank drop of the Hessian at $x^*$.

comments.title: Unjustified coefficient in label noise drift term
comments.quote: $\mathrm{d}Y(t)=-1/4\cdot\partial\Phi(Y(t))\nabla\operatorname{tr}[c\nabla^{2}L(Y(t))]\mathrm{d}t.$

comments.title: Strong conditional assumption on global solution staying in U
comments.quote: If SDE (10) has a global solution $Y$ with $Y(0)=x(0)$ and $Y$ never leaves $U$, i.e., $\mathbb{P}[Y(t)\in U,\forall t\geq 0]=1$, then for any $T>0$, $x_{\eta}(\lfloor T/\eta^{2}\rfloor)$ converges in distribution to $Y(T)$ as $\eta\to 0$.
comments.explanation: The condition $\mathbb{P}[Y(t)\in U,\forall t\geq 0]=1$ is a standard technical requirement in diffusion approximation theorems to ensure the local manifold approximation remains valid throughout the time horizon. However, for SGD dynamics near flat minima, noise can frequently push iterates out of a small neighborhood $U$, particularly if $\Gamma$ is non-compact or the noise covariance is large. Readers will wonder how the convergence rate or error bounds behave if this exit probability is non-negligible in practice. It would be helpful to verify whether the proof accounts for the error introduced by the conditioning event, or to explicitly state that the result is conditional and provide a bound on the deviation when $Y$ exits $U$.
